does: This paper investigates why large language models show degraded safety performance across non-English languages by introducing a Multi-Group Item Response Theory framework that disentangles confounded factors in safety evaluation. Rather than relying on a single Jailbreak Success Rate metric, the authors decompose safety failures into distinct latent variables: language-agnostic safety robustness, intrinsic prompt difficulty, global language processing difficulty, and prompt-specific cross-lingual safety gaps. They evaluate 61 model configurations across 5 model families and 10 languages using the MultiJail dataset of 1.9 million test instances. Their exploratory factor analysis reveals that safety operates primarily through a unidimensional mechanism—models refuse different harm types via shared underlying principles. Surprisingly, the analysis shows 22 model configurations are more vulnerable in English than in low-resource languages, contradicting the intuition that safety necessarily degrades in linguistically underrepresented settings.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature examining language-specific vulnerabilities in large language models, responding to observations that safety guardrails fail differentially across languages. It extends prior jailbreaking research by moving beyond simple success-rate metrics that obscure root causes of failures, drawing methodologically from psychometric measurement theory. The paper connects to work on safety evaluation metrics and automated red-teaming across languages, while engaging with broader questions about alignment and robustness in multilingual systems. It relates to research on language model behavior across diverse linguistic contexts and the challenge of deploying systems equitably across low-resource and high-resource languages.
enables: This framework enables more precise diagnosis of safety failures in multilingual systems, moving beyond surface-level performance metrics to identify specific vulnerabilities. The decomposition of safety-driving factors could guide targeted interventions—whether improving language-agnostic robustness, addressing prompt-specific weaknesses, or enhancing cross-lingual safety transfer. The finding that low-resource languages sometimes show comparable or better safety properties challenges assumptions that guide resource allocation in safety research and suggests that vulnerability patterns may be more nuanced than linguistic representation alone. Future work could leverage this framework to design more effective multilingual safety training, understand mechanistic differences in how models process safety across languages, and develop better strategies for fair safety guarantees across diverse linguistic populations.
